</imports>

<findings>

[observation] [warning] 21 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
[observation] [warning] High coupling (0.56) — 20 imports target "tree-dag-primitives"
[observation] [warning] 13 imports from core back into unit-cli (CLI layer) violate the expected downward dependency direction; these should be refactored so domain code has zero CLI imports.
[observation] [info] At 43 files with 21 entry points this is the densest zone in the codebase; its cohesion of 0.44 is acceptable but trending low — a future decomposition into prd-schema, prd-mutations, and prd-sync sub-zones would improve navigability without breaking the public API.
[observation] [info] The zone correctly anchors the public.ts package boundary and schema/v1.ts canonical types — this is a healthy pattern that makes the external API surface explicit and auditable.
[suggestion] [info] Zone "prd-domain-operations" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
[suggestion] [warning] 21 domain entry points vs 10 CLI entry points is an inverted ratio — audit which domain entry points are accessed only from within the package (not via public.ts) and make them package-private to reduce the exposed API surface
[suggestion] [info] Establish a naming convention for paired sync files: sync-core.ts for algorithm, sync-adapter.ts or sync-cli.ts for the CLI-facing wrapper — the current sync.ts + sync-engine.ts pair is ambiguous about which owns the domain logic
[pattern] [warning] prd-domain-operations has become the de-facto package hub with 150+ combined inbound imports across all zones — this is the single highest architectural risk concentration in the rex package; a formal internal layering (schema → mutations → sync) should be enforced before the zone grows further
[relationship] [warning] The partial extraction of tree-dag-primitives from prd-domain-operations created an inconsistent domain boundary: some primitives (dag.ts, tree.ts, move.ts) are isolated while their consumers remain in the parent zone — the extraction should either complete (merge unit-3 back) or deepen (extract more primitive layers)
[anti-pattern] [info] Zone size of 43 files and 21 entry points is inflated by misclassified CLI command files; do not use these raw numbers to justify architectural refactoring until misclassified files are pinned and re-analyzed
[anti-pattern] [warning] src/cli/commands/add.ts and src/cli/commands/reorganize.ts are [cli-command] files misclassified into the domain zone; their intra-CLI imports to constants.ts, errors.ts, and output.ts appear as 'core → unit-cli' cross-zone violations, inflating the bidirectional coupling metric and overstating zone size by at least 2 files

</findings>

<insights>

- High coupling (0.56) — 20 imports target "tree-dag-primitives"
- 21 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
- public.ts and schema/v1.ts both live here, confirming this zone owns the package's canonical data model and external contract — changes to either ripple across all consumers
- The presence of sync-engine.ts and sync.ts alongside delete/merge/reorganize indicates this zone spans both local mutation and remote-sync concerns, which are distinct responsibilities worth tracking separately as the sync surface grows
- contracts.ts in the store package appearing here suggests the store abstraction boundary is partially blurred into domain logic — verify that store/contracts.ts contains only interface definitions with no implementation
- At 43 files with 21 entry points this is the densest zone in the codebase; its cohesion of 0.44 is acceptable but trending low — a future decomposition into prd-schema, prd-mutations, and prd-sync sub-zones would improve navigability without breaking the public API.
- 13 imports from core back into unit-cli (CLI layer) violate the expected downward dependency direction; these should be refactored so domain code has zero CLI imports.
- The zone correctly anchors the public.ts package boundary and schema/v1.ts canonical types — this is a healthy pattern that makes the external API surface explicit and auditable.
- Zone "prd-domain-operations" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
- The combined fan-in from all consumer zones (unit-cli: 39, cli: 31, unit: 24, validate: 17, store: 18, task-selection: 9, schema-storage: 9, fix: 3) totals over 150 cross-zone imports into a single 43-file zone — this concentration level means any structural change to prd-domain-operations has package-wide blast radius
- The 20 imports from core → unit-3 (tree-dag-primitives) indicate prd-domain-operations has already factored out tree primitives into a satellite zone; this partial extraction creates an asymmetric split where some domain primitives are isolated and others are not, making the boundary inconsistent
- prd-domain-operations has become the de-facto package hub with 150+ combined inbound imports across all zones — this is the single highest architectural risk concentration in the rex package; a formal internal layering (schema → mutations → sync) should be enforced before the zone grows further
- The partial extraction of tree-dag-primitives from prd-domain-operations created an inconsistent domain boundary: some primitives (dag.ts, tree.ts, move.ts) are isolated while their consumers remain in the parent zone — the extraction should either complete (merge unit-3 back) or deepen (extract more primitive layers)
- src/cli/commands/add.ts and src/cli/commands/reorganize.ts carry the [cli-command] archetype yet are classified inside this domain zone by community detection; their imports from constants.ts, errors.ts, and output.ts are the primary source of the apparent 'core → unit-cli' bidirectional coupling — correcting this misclassification eliminates most of the perceived domain→CLI violations without any code changes
- The stated 43-file size includes at least two misclassified CLI command files, meaning the true domain zone is smaller; re-running zone analysis after pinning add.ts and reorganize.ts to rex-cli-command-layer will produce more accurate cohesion and coupling baselines
- src/cli/commands/add.ts and src/cli/commands/reorganize.ts are [cli-command] files misclassified into the domain zone; their intra-CLI imports to constants.ts, errors.ts, and output.ts appear as 'core → unit-cli' cross-zone violations, inflating the bidirectional coupling metric and overstating zone size by at least 2 files
- Zone size of 43 files and 21 entry points is inflated by misclassified CLI command files; do not use these raw numbers to justify architectural refactoring until misclassified files are pinned and re-analyzed
- sync-engine.ts and sync.ts coexist in this zone with no naming convention distinguishing the CLI adapter from the core implementation — the dual-file pattern without a suffix convention (e.g., -core.ts vs -cli.ts) makes it ambiguous which file owns sync logic and which wraps it for CLI output
- The 21 entry points include public.ts, which is the intentional aggregation point for the package API — counting it as one of 21 entry points conflates the public API module with internal entry points and overstates entry point sprawl by one
- prd-domain-operations has 21 entry points while rex-cli-command-layer has 10 — the domain layer exposes twice as many entry points as the CLI layer; a healthy layered architecture typically has the opposite ratio, indicating domain internals are being accessed directly rather than through a stable narrow public API
- Establish a naming convention for paired sync files: sync-core.ts for algorithm, sync-adapter.ts or sync-cli.ts for the CLI-facing wrapper — the current sync.ts + sync-engine.ts pair is ambiguous about which owns the domain logic
- 21 domain entry points vs 10 CLI entry points is an inverted ratio — audit which domain entry points are accessed only from within the package (not via public.ts) and make them package-private to reduce the exposed API surface
- [call graph] 1240 internal calls, 156 outgoing, 108 incoming (cohesion: 0.89, coupling: 0.11)

</insights>
